title: Test Your Understanding: Scalability
description: See how well you understood vertical vs horizontal scaling and statelessness!

questions.question: What best describes vertical scaling?

options.text: Adding more machines behind a load balancer
Giving a single machine more CPU, RAM, or faster disks
Splitting the database across many nodes
Caching responses at the edge

questions.explanation: Vertical scaling (scaling up) means making one machine more powerful. Adding more machines is horizontal scaling (scaling out).

questions.question: What is the biggest limitation of vertical scaling?

options.text: It requires rewriting your application
It has a hard ceiling and remains a single point of failure
It only works with NoSQL databases
It cannot be done in the cloud

questions.explanation: There is a biggest machine you can buy, it gets disproportionately expensive, and if that one box fails the whole system is down. Notably, vertical scaling usually needs NO code changes, which is its main appeal.

questions.question: Horizontal scaling generally requires your application to be designed for it, while vertical scaling usually does not.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Vertical scaling is mostly a config change. Horizontal scaling requires interchangeable servers, which means your app must not depend on per-server state.

questions.question: Why does storing user sessions in a server's local memory break horizontal scaling?

options.text: Memory is slower than disk
A later request may hit a different server that has no record of that session
Load balancers cannot read cookies
Sessions are encrypted and cannot be shared

questions.explanation: With multiple servers, a user's requests can land on different machines. If the session only lives in one server's memory, the others see the user as logged out.

questions.question: What makes a service 'stateless'?

options.text: It uses no database at all
It keeps no per-user data in the process between requests, so any server can handle any request
It never caches anything
It only handles read requests

questions.explanation: Stateless means the server holds no per-request/per-user state itself. State lives in shared stores, so requests are interchangeable across servers. The system still has state, it just lives elsewhere.

questions.question: Where should state typically live so that app servers can stay stateless? (Select all that apply)

options.text: A shared cache like Redis for sessions
A database for durable data
Object storage like S3 for uploaded files
The local memory of whichever server handled the request

questions.explanation: Sessions go in a shared cache, durable data in a database, and large files in object storage. Local process memory or local disk is exactly what breaks horizontal scaling.

questions.question: Sticky sessions (session affinity) are the recommended permanent solution to running a stateful app across many servers.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Sticky sessions pin a user to one server. They hurt even load distribution and still lose that user's state when the server dies. Making the service stateless is the real fix.

questions.question: After you scale your stateless app tier out to many nodes, what commonly becomes the next bottleneck?

options.text: The load balancer's cookie parser
The shared resource they all depend on, often the database
The number of available IP addresses
The HTML rendering engine

questions.explanation: Scaling out the easy, stateless tier just pushes the pressure to the shared resource behind it, typically the database, which then needs its own scaling strategy (replication, sharding).
